# loki — Copilot Instructions

## Project Overview

loki (Local Offline Knowledge Index) is a Python CLI tool that manages a self-hosted, fully offline AI stack. It combines Ollama (local LLM inference), Open WebUI (chat interface), and Kiwix (offline Wikipedia and other ZIM archives), orchestrated via Docker Compose. Ollama runs as a native host service (not in Docker); everything else runs in containers. A single `loki setup` command installs all prerequisites and configures the stack from `config.yaml`.

## Architecture

- `loki/cli.py` — Click CLI entry point: `setup`, `start`, `stop`, `status`, `cleanup`
- `loki/config.py` — Pydantic config models, path helpers, and file generators for `Caddyfile` and `.env`
- `loki/system.py` — All subprocess-backed system operations: package installation, Docker/Ollama install, systemd override for Ollama network binding, shell profile editing, mDNS announcement via `avahi-publish-address`
- `config.yaml` — User-edited source of truth for URL, ports, ZIM files, and Ollama models
- `compose.yaml` — Docker Compose stack definition (Open WebUI, Kiwix, Caddy)
- `tools/kiwix_tool.py` — Open WebUI tool definition (Python class) for querying the Kiwix server; paste into Admin Panel → Tools to expose offline archives to the LLM
- `data/kiwix/` — ZIM files downloaded by `loki setup`
- `Caddyfile` and `.env` — Generated by `loki setup`; never edited by hand

## Code Conventions

- Python 3.11+; use modern type hints (`list[str]`, `Path | None`)
- Pydantic v2 for config validation (`model_validate`)
- CLI output goes to stdout via `click.echo`; errors use `click.echo(..., err=True)`
- `subprocess.run(..., check=False)` for external commands; handle non-zero returns explicitly
- No logging framework — keep output minimal and user-facing

## Build and Test

```bash
pip install -e ".[dev]"
pytest
```

Tests live in `tests/` and use `pytest-mock`. The `sample_config` fixture in `conftest.py` provides a standard `LokiConfig` for all tests. Mock `subprocess.run`, `requests.get`, and file I/O rather than hitting real services.
